text: Understanding the interaction between nanocarriers and plasma proteins is vital for predicting their biological fate, yet the distinction between the "hard" and "soft" protein corona remains analytically challenging. In this study, we provide a complementary characterization of the protein corona formed on hydroxyethyl starch (HES) nanocapsules with various surface functionalizations. To investigate the hard corona, we utilized sodium dodecyl sulphate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) and protein quantification assays, which revealed consistent adsorption patterns across different capsule types but showed a notable depletion of human serum albumin (HSA). In contrast, the soft corona was analyzed in situ using isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and dynamic light scattering (DLS). These techniques demonstrated that HSA actually adsorbs in high quantities but with low binding affinity, identifying it as a primary component of the soft corona. Conversely, apolipoprotein A-I (ApoA-I) exhibited high binding affinities despite low stoichiometric adsorption, confirming its role in the hard corona. Our findings highlight that the perceived composition of the protein corona is heavily dependent on the isolation and characterization techniques employed. We conclude that a multi-methodological approach is essential to accurately define the biological identity of nanomaterials for therapeutic applications.
